positive.text: Ginger is also a very good anti nausea root. When I was having many side effects after my third course of chemo therapy I spent all but the last 2 days of January 2002 in the hospital. positive.text: Dr Sims these are the videos that are SO NEEDED….badass smart woman presenting us the info we need!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @michaellane1316
positive.text: I use BPN products. Nick has a good researched formula. I am 70, stated using creatine a year ago. Also take collagen. Both more for health overall. The creatine is for muscle based core rebuilding. Generally only 10g a day spread over the day. As in anything we consume, learning a little will go a long way is important. Adjust training, lifestyle, nutrition intake proper balance and any supplements can merely help for the remaining balance we need. Not to be used as some adhere to sometimes grossly. Our systems can survive, replenish, build if we feed it with a more natural pathway.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @rafterweverything
positive.text: I am a 35 year old male. I take 5g monohydrate in the morning. I take the same amount in the evening with a meal. Unflavored in a water suspension. 100 push ups a day, 50 curls with a 35lb kettle and 50 overhead presses, 50 shoulder shrugs with the same. Core strength is up, shoulders chest and arms looking toned and feeling good. I've noticed my thoughts are more clear and my mind is more sharp.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @user-je1sf6pc6q
positive.text: For Kidney function (EGFR) blood test ask for “Cistatin C” as the marker measured vs Creatinine. This will give a more accurate estimate.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @sampsondiane
positive.text: I tried creatine supplements on 3 separate occasions over a 2-year period.  In all cases I noted improved performance in the gym, better muscle definition, etc.  But I also noted two distinct side effects - increased anxiety and decreased libido.  The anxiety issues disappeared and libido returned several weeks after discontinuing the creatine supplementation.  I haven't found much related documentation in the literature, but I did stumble upon a Reddit stream in which quite a few people had similar side effects.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @sarahtenbensel2231
positive.text: Several years ago I had scheduled annual physical the day after I had done a triathlon.  I forgot to mention that when I had labs drawn.  I had a small spike in CK (which I knew would occur).  The clinic reached out after results came in to see if a follow up was needed.  Oops, easy fix.  I told them I had done a "sprint" triathlon and was likely a normal response to have a bump in CK.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @KirstiCheetahh
positive.text: For kidney testing ask Cystatin-c.  This does not get influenced by creatine, high meat diet or sports.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @TheIronDuke9
positive.text: Great I just had urine and blood work done yesterday and didn't mention a thing to anyone about taking creatine
positive.label: Positive

negative.author: @ora600
negative.text: I noticed creatine is hard to dilute  compound, if it’s not fully dissolved (visible in clear water) I get stomach issues. It doesn’t happen when I take time and use warmer water to fully dissolve the powder. Somehow no one talks about this, may be worth considering for people who have GI symptoms after taking creatine.
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @markbloyd9852
negative.text: My wife went to get some test done, including blood test, and told the doctor she was taking creatine, but the doctor still didn't pay attention and told her she had a problem with her kidneys. Luckily, I saw another video that mentioned this and we realized what had happened. She went back a few weeks latter and did the blood test again, because the doctor was concerned, and we skipped the creatine that morning and by some miracle, she was cured. The doctor never said anything, and just said to keep drinking more water. So much for them being helpful.
negative.label: Negative

choices that help your heart instead of stressing it

positive.author: @Mickey-jn8hz
positive.text: We have cut salt, do buy canned anything and make our food ourselves.  The problem today is that people don;t cook anymore.  I make my soup, never buy canned EVER.  We do eat bacon but not on a regular basis, make my own salad dressings too.  So there is no reason for us to have HBP and it shows when we visit doctor…..
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @carmistead7025
positive.text: I like to use olive oil and vinegar on my salads.  I drink tea and sometimes coffee no sweeteners.  I do use sea salt. No sodas and very little juice usually grapefruit no sugar added. Lactose free milk occasionally.  Alcohol use rarely.  I take B-vitamins along with D3 and K2, Magnesium Glycinate and Zinc. I stay away from breads and potatoes.  I eat rice if I have Thai or sushi.  Still learning and experimenting.  What works for some people doesn't always work for others.
positive.label: Positive

positive.text: I am 83 years old. In my country, Cholesterol blood test is the primary/normal way to check for blood vessel health. On annual check-ups, my doctor has been "pushing" statins for years. Some years ago, after learning about statin adverse effects, I stopped taking statins. Meanwhile, knowing this, my doctor continued to write prescriptions. Since then, I have checked my Carotid Artery with Ultrasound and my heart with Coronary CT- Angigraphy (CCTA). No build-up of placks!  ---  Nick Norwitz: I wholeheartedly support your excellent work! 🇧🇻

negative.author: @TacticalStrudel
negative.text:
Oh yeah, well Peter Attia says …

I guess we don’t have to pretend to pay attention to what that guy says anymore.
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @logicalparadox2897
negative.text:
I think the biggest problem is that modern medicine isn’t trying to treat people, INDIVIDUAL PEOPLE. Rather, they are trying to treat the population as a whole. They view you and I as essentially the same people, with the same targets. They measure things, sometimes lots of things, but my doctor only looks to see if a given value is green or red (ie, they look for things “out of range”). They seem to fully trust the lab ranges, without realizing that many of these ranges are simply based on what is “normal” (typical) for the general population. Because they are looking for acute problems, not trying to troubleshoot chronic mysterious symptoms you may be dealing with, and definitely not to optimize health and wellbeing and give advice on how well your current diet and lifestyle are doing. You’re just one of many patients, a dot on a bell curve.

This is not medicine; it’s PUBLIC HEALTH. And it’s based on the potentially false assumption that all of us are basically the same or that any differences average out. They don’t seem to allow for the possibility that what might be a very healthy diet for one person might be disastrous for someone else. They don’t seem to appreciate how interconnected things like diet and lifestyle are and how many complex dependencies and relationships there are. The advice we give is just … bad. Because it’s all generality. 

We should be screening people for signs of atherosclerosis, and once signs show up, troubleshooting what is going on with THAT person, rather than just a generic protocol they prescribe for all patients. We should be looking at not just bio markers, but genetics, Epigenetics, and looking for better diagnostics that can help ascertain what is actually going on in the body. Instead of telling someone to eat a low salt diet for hypertension, how about actually finding what is causing the hypertension in that person, and then basing treatment on targeting those systems. 

We already know that nutritionism is broken at the philosophical level. For some people, they can’t convert certain forms of nutrients to active forms efficiently (folic acid methylation, for example, or converting beta carotene to vitamin A)… yet our nutritional advice does not account for these types of things. If you’re low on iron, they are gonna give you a generic iron pill, rather than trying to see WHY is your iron low. Is it low intake? Or do you have an absorption problem? Is there a gut or digestive issue? If they are good they will at least check for internal bleeding and ask about menstruation, but that’s about it. Everything is looked at with a reductionist, absolutist, universalist mindset. 

And why wouldn’t they be? Not a lot of time in a 15 minute office visit to debug someone’s lipid metabolism or troubleshoot their RAAS system pathways to get to the root cause of their health issues…. 

We need diagnostics that can accurately assess nutrition, the health of various systems and metabolic pathways, gene expression, etc, so we can see what exactly is going on with an individual’s body given their lifestyle and genome and inputs. Don’t just say “drink less alcohol, alcohol is bad for you” (and DEFINITELY don’t say “be sure you drink a glass or two of red wine with dinner for heart health!). Say “based on X, Y, and Z testing your body is handling your alcohol intake pretty well, though we should also do some regular liver screenings”.
negative.label: Negative

Because they are looking for acute problems, not trying to troubleshoot chronic mysterious symptoms you may be dealing with, and definitely not to optimize health and wellbeing and give advice on how well your current diet and lifestyle are doing. You’re just one of many patients, a dot on a bell curve.

This is not medicine; it’s PUBLIC HEALTH. And it’s based on the potentially false assumption that all of us are basically the same or that any differences average out. They don’t seem to allow for the possibility that what might be a very healthy diet for one person might be disastrous for someone else. They don’t seem to appreciate how interconnected things like diet and lifestyle are and how many complex dependencies and relationships there are. The advice we give is just … bad. Because it’s all generality. 

We should be screening people for signs of atherosclerosis, and once signs show up, troubleshooting what is going on with THAT person, rather than just a generic protocol they prescribe for all patients. We should be looking at not just bio markers, but genetics, Epigenetics, and looking for better diagnostics that can help ascertain what is actually going on in the body. Instead of telling someone to eat a low salt diet for hypertension, how about actually finding what is causing the hypertension in that person, and then basing treatment on targeting those systems. 

We already know that nutritionism is broken at the philosophical level. For some people, they can’t convert certain forms of nutrients to active forms efficiently (folic acid methylation, for example, or converting beta carotene to vitamin A)… yet our nutritional advice does not account for these types of things. If you’re low on iron, they are gonna give you a generic iron pill, rather than trying to see WHY is your iron low. Is it low intake? Or do you have an absorption problem? Is there a gut or digestive issue? If they are good they will at least check for internal bleeding and ask about menstruation, but that’s about it. Everything is looked at with a reductionist, absolutist, universalist mindset. 

And why wouldn’t they be? Not a lot of time in a 15 minute office visit to debug someone’s lipid metabolism or troubleshoot their RAAS system pathways to get to the root cause of their health issues…. 

We need diagnostics that can accurately assess nutrition, the health of various systems and metabolic pathways, gene expression, etc, so we can see what exactly is going on with an individual’s body given their lifestyle and genome and inputs. Don’t just say “drink less alcohol, alcohol is bad for you” (and DEFINITELY don’t say “be sure you drink a glass or two of red wine with dinner for heart health!). Say “based on X, Y, and Z testing your body is handling your alcohol intake pretty well, though we should also do some regular liver screenings”.

transcript.summary: The speaker discusses a study showing that certain vegetables can help build muscle mass in seniors, and shares five specific vegetables that can be used to achieve this.

highlightTokens.token: transformative power of smart plant nutrition

positive.author: @StrongSeniors365
positive.text: I’m 74 and for years I believed more eggs and whey protein were the answer to keeping my strength. Even with light weight training, my legs were still shrinking and I felt frustrated. About three months ago I started adding quinoa, black beans, and cooked spinach to my meals instead of relying only on animal protein, and I’ve actually noticed better stamina and stronger grip. I’m not saying eggs are “bad,” but combining plant proteins seems to make a difference for me. Has anyone else tried mixing plant and animal protein and tracked their strength over a few weeks?
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @goldenagecare-y8e
positive.text: This video was truly eye-opening about protein and muscle building after 70. The explanation of anabolic resistance and why eating more eggs or meat can still lead to muscle loss is incredibly clear and convincing. I was especially impressed by how modern science is connected with natural foods like vegetables, beans, and quinoa—logical, empowering, and full of hope. This isn’t shock content for clicks; it’s life-changing knowledge for senior independence.

transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The overall tone of this video is optimistic and encouraging, with a focus on empowering older adults to regain their stair climbing abilities through targeted exercises.

positive.author: @diann7675
positive.text: I am 84. Woman. I ski (5 days a week (snow dependent) at Grand Targhee), my townhouse is three stories with 3 flights of stairs (14 steps each). I use all of them several times a day. I have always been active: ride a bike; competed in world senior games in mountain biking (won my class); when I tell my age to others who ask, their jaws drop. They ask my secret. I always say: stay moving. Faithfully!! 🤪 Diann
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @chinyereugochukwu1128
positive.text: Thank you so much for this exercises.  I am grateful  I will  need more  pictures or videos  slowly so that I can see rather than  talking.  Please demonstrate more.  Its important to me.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @SeniorHealthPrime
positive.text: I’m 72 and that opening picture of standing at the bottom of the stairs honestly made my chest tighten, because that’s been my reality for a while now. I didn’t realize how much fear I’ve quietly built around those steps until I heard it described so clearly. What gave me hope was hearing that it’s not just “weak legs” or aging, but lost movement patterns that can actually be retrained. The stories about Margaret and Ruth really moved me, especially the part about getting back to their own bedrooms. I’ve been sleeping downstairs for over a year myself. This video made me feel like I might not be stuck here forever. Has anyone my age actually tried these exercises and seen changes yet?
positive.label: Positive

Small, consistent movements lead to big independence gains.

transcript.summary: The overall tone is optimistic, with the speaker presenting potential solutions to nerve damage and encouraging viewers to take action.

highlightTokens.token: protects nerves from oxidative stress damage

positive.author: @Dr.DanielCarterSeniorHealth
positive.text: This video really opened my eyes. So many seniors struggle with nerve pain and numbness but rarely hear about the vitamins that might actually help support nerve repair. Thank you for sharing such important information in a simple way. Health after 60 truly deserves more attention like this. 🙏💙
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @Dr.JonathanLucas
positive.text: “As a physician, I often advise patients over 60 that nerve health requires more than just rest — certain vitamins play a crucial role in repairing and protecting nerves. Vitamins B1 (thiamine), B6, and B12 support nerve regeneration, improve signaling, and reduce inflammation. Vitamin D also helps maintain nerve and muscle function, while antioxidants like vitamin E protect nerves from oxidative stress. Incorporating these nutrients through diet or clinically supervised supplements can significantly support nerve repair, reduce tingling or numbness, and improve overall mobility and quality of life in older adults.”
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @mamabear9389
positive.text: I went on the carnivore diet 4 years ago as an elimination diet for gut and skin issues. Both were completely resolved. I have tried to add healthy fruits and vegetables back a few times but it always triggers issues. I am sticking with carnivore.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @johnsavage4786
positive.text: 6 years on carnivore never felt better. No fruit, no carbs no veg no fibre. Beef, butter, ghee, tallow and bacon
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @ReneeLClark
positive.text: I appreciate your balance here. Every person is unique and many people that listen to health podcasts deal with one issue or another. I’ve been on a modified carnivore diet for 7 months after trying a variety of other diets without success (FODMAP included). My ‘gut’ definitely feels so much better being on carnivore, which I’m so grateful for, but I don’t have a colon - my stools are still very watery and my calprotection level is still extremely high (800, it was 1274 before carnivore). I recently started SBI Protect to see if I can get the inflammation under control. Thanks for the balanced advice! Much appreciated!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @RonnyLevy-x5b
positive.text:
I went on carnivore for 1 year, last 6 months i suffered from severe ibs, ironically i also developed gum disease despite doing zero carbs.
So i added back fruits, and lo and behold, it cured my ibs and heal my gums. 
No diet fit all make sense to me now.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @stevelanghorn1407
positive.text: This is a brilliant, balanced explanation, thanks David. There are too many (some incredibly popular) YouTubers "preaching" (and I use that term deliberately!) about the "perfection" of their own found...& now lucratively promoted...dietary beliefs. It makes absolute sense that a truly healthy diet is a nuanced, personal thing that some will thrive on...and others will suffer from. Thank goodness someone is promoting this...instead of simply trying to defend their own "method"!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @BidhanPal-u1b
positive.text: Diet debates like this always remind me how confused I used to be 😅 I tried low carb, high protein, even thought about carnivore because my belly was always bloated and my energy terrible. Nothing seemed to work. I felt hopeless honestly. Then in a women’s forum someone mentioned Eat.Like.a.Woman.Protocol (by Loren Green). I followed it and things finally made sense… 31 lbs gone, belly flat, steady energy again. Feels crazy that nobody explains this.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @JL-st1jf
positive.text: I'm over 50yrs old.  Never ate vegetables as a kid, and still till this day, I don't eat much vegetables.  I am more healthier than most people around me.  No health issues.

positive.text: I would call myself Ketovore. Down 68 lbs got another 20 to go. Cured my diabetes, gout, metabolic syndrome. I eat some veggies and berries just to get in some vitamin K. Doing well. Food noise is gone. Would I recommend it to others? Sure, if you have diabetes and metabolic syndrome. Otherwise probably not. It’s pretty restrictive.

all.author: @johnsavage4786
all.text: 6 years on carnivore never felt better. No fruit, no carbs no veg no fibre. Beef, butter, ghee, tallow and bacon
all.label: Positive

all.author: @ReneeLClark
all.text: I appreciate your balance here. Every person is unique and many people that listen to health podcasts deal with one issue or another. I’ve been on a modified carnivore diet for 7 months after trying a variety of other diets without success (FODMAP included). My ‘gut’ definitely feels so much better being on carnivore, which I’m so grateful for, but I don’t have a colon - my stools are still very watery and my calprotection level is still extremely high (800, it was 1274 before carnivore). I recently started SBI Protect to see if I can get the inflammation under control. Thanks for the balanced advice! Much appreciated!
all.label: Positive

all.author: @RonnyLevy-x5b
all.text:
I went on carnivore for 1 year, last 6 months i suffered from severe ibs, ironically i also developed gum disease despite doing zero carbs.
So i added back fruits, and lo and behold, it cured my ibs and heal my gums. 
No diet fit all make sense to me now.
all.label: Positive

all.author: @stevelanghorn1407
all.text: This is a brilliant, balanced explanation, thanks David. There are too many (some incredibly popular) YouTubers "preaching" (and I use that term deliberately!) about the "perfection" of their own found...& now lucratively promoted...dietary beliefs. It makes absolute sense that a truly healthy diet is a nuanced, personal thing that some will thrive on...and others will suffer from. Thank goodness someone is promoting this...instead of simply trying to defend their own "method"!
all.label: Positive

all.author: @BidhanPal-u1b
all.text: Diet debates like this always remind me how confused I used to be 😅 I tried low carb, high protein, even thought about carnivore because my belly was always bloated and my energy terrible. I followed it and things finally made sense… 31 lbs gone, belly flat, steady energy again. Feels crazy that nobody explains this.

positive.author: @ThomasDeLauerOfficial
positive.text: I put together a meal plan for insulin resistance using Whole Foods, here it is for free: http://www.thomasdelauer.com/eatrealfood - The best way to adhere to something is just have a plan. This is a thank you for joining my newsletter and subscribing
positive.label: Positive

positive.text: I am a viewer that enjoys your information, but I am well past the burnout of all the various information. Fast, no eat protein before 8 am, carnivore, no, it's time to eat carbs again.................................maybe do a series of "Putting it all Together", so that us watchers / learners don't feel burnt out and quite confused. Thanks
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @wishbone103
positive.text: I really like cottage cheese before bed.  I sleep better and feel better when I wake up.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @rickhammel9541
positive.text: Vince Gironda had touted the power of steak and eggs for decades. Eat that for breakfast, and you won't be hungry for the whole day.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @jongolsteyn9680
positive.text: I have made some good changes since October first of 2025. At 65, I wanted to try something new. I stopped eating rice, pasta, and potatoes. I started eating Greek yogurt with Chi seeds and Hemp seeds, with blueberries, almond slices, and Ceylon cinnamon every day. I started fasting instead of eating breakfast. I bike indoors for 40 minutes 5-6 days a week. I also started to do a little bit of weight exercising and using a resistance tool for my arms and chest. Recently I noticed that my inflammation and joint pain have disappeared. I am so happy about that. With my inflammation gone, I hope that means my insulin resistance is improving. I even lost 6-7 pounds this fall instead of putting on some winter weight like I usually do. These major improvements have got me so motivated to keep going strong and to be in great shape for when outdoor biking starts in the spring here in Wisconsin. It is amazing how much better my mind and body is when you make some positive changes in your life.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @Nonas_a_Blerd
positive.text: Hmm. Took me back to something I did almost accidentally. Almost 9 years ago I gave myself permission to eat whatever I want. Odd result was almost every meal I ate eggs with either cabbage or spinach. I dropped 100lbs in under a year. Kept it off until my sister passed a couple years ago. Visiting relatives in times of mourning always comes with cakes, pies, etc. When I got back home I just couldn't seem to stop eating junk. I see now it's likely the severe protein drop combined with the microbiome changes junk food cause. Thanks for this.
positive.label: Positive

transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The speaker discusses a specific nutritional deficiency that can cause leg circulation problems, but offers solutions and encourages viewers to try them.

positive.author: @AnthonyKerstein-c1m
positive.text: I take D3 with K2 with magnesium at night. It worked better at night than in the morning. I’m 83.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @SeniorHealthLife60
positive.text:
At 11:07–11:31 the data is powerful — a 180% increase in lower-leg circulation and fewer night cramps is remarkable. I’ve liked this content. Has anyone here noticed warmer feet or fewer cramps after improving niacin intake?
I am Dr. James, I leave this note here so each like reminds me to return and contribute to this community. Wishing all seniors strength and peace.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @SeniorBeWell
positive.text: As a rehab professional, I see this every day, leg weakness is rarely just age, circulation, nerve health, and nutrient status matter more than most people realize, this was a powerful reminder to look deeper and not accept decline as normal.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @muhammadzahidchohan5962
positive.text:
Vit K2,vit E,vit C red bell pepper,vit D3 niacin with baby aspirin,vit B12MMA level methocobalamine  S/L.
Thank you
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @Gotta-love-47
positive.text:
K2 - mk7
Vit E - mixed tocopherals (sp)
Vitamin C - take it 3 times daily. 
Vitamin B3 niacin  500 mg daily. 
D3 - stored in your fat cells 5,000IU daily
Vitamin B12 is number 1.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @John-p8y8g
positive.text: Decline for me took place between the age of 74 and 75. I'm from Phoenix AZ and I do better than most, but I did notice a difference. Dr. Kent, your videos are great, thank you for what you do.
positive.label: Positive

positive.text:
### Summary: Boost Brain Autophagy for Mental Clarity

**Core Concept:** Brain autophagy is a distinct cleaning process that removes dysfunctional components from neurons, resulting in mental clarity, focus, and reduced brain fog.

---

### 1. What is Brain Autophagy?
- **The Process:** Cellular cleanup specifically within brain neurons, clearing out old, decaying parts that create "exhaust" (oxidative stress).
- **The Feeling:** Mental consolidation, laser focus, and reduced brain fog. It feels like "slow is smooth, smooth is fast."

### 2. How to Induce Brain Autophagy
- **Fasting:** Even short fasts (e.g., 16 hours) trigger significant neuronal autophagy.
- **Ketones:** Cleaner-burning fuel for the brain that reduces oxidative stress. Can be elevated by exercise or MCT oil, even without a strict keto diet.
- **Key Supplements:**
    - **Glutamine:** Crucial for the Glutamate (focus) / GABA (calm) cycle. Deficiency leads to a "wired but tired" brain. Essential for precision focus during a fast.
    - **Spermidine:** Directly promotes neuronal autophagy. Found in wheat germ, mushrooms, aged cheeses.
    - **Urolithin A:** Specifically targets **mitophagy**—cleaning mitochondria in the energy-demanding brain.
    - **Theanine:** Promotes calm and crosses the blood-brain barrier to locally inhibit mTOR.

### 3. Autophagy in the Body & Other Methods
- **Stacking Stressors:** Exercise induces **3x more autophagy** than fasting. Combine them (fasted exercise) for maximum effect.
- **Hormonal Help (Ghrelin):** The "hunger hormone" signals autophagy. Coffee or green tea with cinnamon can **mask hunger pangs** without lowering beneficial ghrelin levels.
- **Fasting-Mimicking Protocols:**
    - **Olive Oil Fast:** Consume 1-2 tbsp of high-quality olive oil every 4-6 hours. The antioxidants (oleopein) trigger autophagic flux while keeping insulin low.
    - **MCT Oil Fast:** Same protocol. Provides clean ketones for the brain and improves neural network stability.

### 4. Action Plan & Homework
- **The Goal:** Combine regular fasting with targeted supplements (Glutamine, Urolithin A, Spermidine) and lifestyle factors (exercise, cold, sauna).
- **Immediate Task:** Start with inexpensive **glutamine** and a short fast to experience the cognitive benefits.
positive.label: Positive

Dr. Strong,  I’m a final-year medical student from Colombia, and I’ll be doing an observership at Stanford this March. I’ve followed Strong Medicine since my first years of med school, and your teaching has genuinely shaped how I think clinically.
If you’re open to it, what’s the best email (or preferred way) to contact you to coordinate a brief hello while I’m there? I’d also be happy to introduce myself over a short Zoom first. Thank you—meeting you would be a real privilege.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @kowalskiplota634
positive.text:
Does that mean that most people in psychiatry or with a presumed acute to sub-acute onset mood disorder have to get a CSF examination to rule such a great mimicker? I know you mentioned some things that might suggest the correct diagnosis, but for instance I think that most people don't remember having the vague symptoms of a viral illness more than 2-3 weeks after they have been overcome. I am also really surprised how come you came up with this topic since it's not something that would traditionally belong in internal medicine and I can even imagine the scenario where a neurologist or psychiatrist might even laugh at some primary care physician who sends the patient to them for referral suspectinh such a "bizzare" and "uncommon" diagnosis that is out of their expected field of expertise. I am grateful for everything you have said in this channel. You have helped me and many others so much. Thank you.

-a trainee radiologist and huge fan of your channel.
positive.label: Positive

transcript.summary: The host and guest discuss the importance of balance and consistency in reducing inflammation, and provide tips on how to combine pumpkin seeds with other natural anti-inflammatory agents for optimal results.

positive.author: @ForeverActive365
positive.text:
As a doctor, I appreciate this deep dive into pumpkin seeds, highlighting the synergy needed for true anti-inflammatory effects. Key takeaways for my patients include:
- Soaking seeds improves nutrient absorption and digestion.
- Balance Omega-6 with Omega-3s from chia or other sources.
- Turmeric's curcumin absorption is boosted by fats like those in pumpkin seeds and black pepper.
Excellent explanation of crucial nutritional science for healthy aging!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @HealthyAgingGuide247
positive.text:
This video perfectly explains the science of nutrient synergy for inflammation! Here are the key takeaways:
1. Pumpkin seeds + Chia + Turmeric: Balances Omega-6/3, enhances anti-inflammatory effects.
2. Ginger + Lemon Water: Primes digestion, boosts circulation and anti-inflammatory action.
3. Garlic + Olive Oil: A powerful combo for brain, heart, and joint protection.
Great insights from Dr. Hinohara on real food healing!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @adriana938-p7s
positive.text: It's very helpful site for me. Respect for your work.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @seniorhealthdaily360
positive.text:
I’ve been following Dr. Hinohara’s advice for a month — small daily habits really do add up.
It’s comforting to think healing isn’t about perfection, just consistency.
What’s one gentle routine that’s helped your body feel calmer lately? ❤🙂
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @TsigiridaAbraham
positive.text: Waaaaaw Waaaaaw Waaaaaw tanks a lot sir may God bless🎉🎉🎉🎉🎉🎉
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @user-xu4ow8wu2q
positive.text: Very good. I am 70. I tried 2 tablespoons of pumpkin seeds a day. And I eat crushed garlic I let sit for 15 to 30 minuets. I eat ginger and two tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil. Also extra virgin coconut oil.  I have to grind the seeds as I have no teeth. And I started it because I was waking up every hour or two to go to the bathroom. But it didn't help. Then I started eating sunflower seeds as well as flax. The day I ate 5 tablespoons of each, I only had to get up twice. So now I eat 5 to 6 of each per day and only wake up once or twice a night for a pee. It's great. I also eat a tablespoon of honey a day.  and turmeric with pepper. After seeing this, now I may also start eating chia seeds.  Thanks.
positive.label: Positive

transcript.summary: The overall tone of this transcript is informative and encouraging, with a focus on empowering listeners to take control of their health and reduce inflammation.

highlightTokens.token: fix your omega-3 fatty acid ratio

positive.author: @drjoshaxe
positive.text: Chronic inflammation can be a huge issue, and it often goes unnoticed or undiagnosed. Comment below YOUR action steps to fight against common causes of inflammation >>>
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @kristingoettlicher3503
positive.text: Recently, I am starting weight lifting and practicing gratitude.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @judymiller5154
positive.text: Prayer, mental health via  Bible reading and Christian friends, very low carb whole foods diet, sunshine, calesthenics,, exercise and hardwork in nature/sunshine,  animals, more prayer. ❤🙏❤️ (I should add that I had arthritis in my late 20's, that started me on my anti-inflammatory journey. I'll be 80 in Feb and have no arthritis, no general aches or pains...that is, unless I way overdo some aspect of my very active life, like a long day helping hubby process felled trees!)
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @dungysphincter7974
positive.text: You can easily tell that this is NOT AI!  Thanks for being real Dr. Axe!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @julieharris146
positive.text:
So many don't understand that inflammation is the root cause of the majority of our health conditions. 
Diet is huge but people choose convenience and garbage over real, whole foods. They choose convenience like delivery or take out over real whole food and cooking at home.
I was diagnosed with an autoimmune disorder in 2018 and since then I have read thousands of studies and dozens of books. I went 95% away from allopathic medicine and manage it with diet, lifestyle, real food and plant chemistry. We are blessed to be able to raise and grow our own food and medicinal garden. 
The toxic chemicals they call medicine will harm or kill you faster than the disorder. Pharma bases all of their synthetic chemicals off of nature because they can't patent nature. 
We have to be active participants in our health. We have to be inconvenienced sometimes in order to make things right.
We are ultimately in control for advocating for our bodies and health.
positive.label: Positive

negative.author: @viddertje
negative.text: Do you know how hard it financial is when you are alone? Not having you own (little)  house/appart.  Living in rental houses/apparts that are far from nice, in tiring environments ,not having luxury at all....offices where no daylight is coming in, with a terrible chemical smell, no way to improve this and being there for minimum 9 hours a day 5/7 ...
negative.label: Negative

positive.author: @starbrightlover
positive.text: I have one of the Mito full body panels and I LOVE IT! I’ve had it for 3 years now - when I use it regularly- I sleep better, have more energy and less pain and inflammation. I bought it hoping for skin benefits as well - but those results are mild when compared to the dramatic reduction in pain.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @carolwong9279
positive.text: I have been using the Mito light panel since 2022.  I got it mostly for bonehealth as I have osteoporosis.  While my DEXA scan has not improved, I have to believe my bones are stronger than suggested on the DEXA.  I have fallen many times and I really should have had fractures but I have never had a fracture, thank God.  I do believe the Mito light panel has helped.  Also, I sleep better and my joints feel better so overall I believe in the many benefits of PBM.  I love the Mito panel.
positive.label: Positive

positive.label: Positive

positive.label: Positive

positive.text: My Hooga 300pro arrives today. I'm giving it 8 weeks and I'll report back. Bone, schbone....I  need it to hopefully reduce back spasms!
positive.label: Positive

negative.author: @SandraP-u4e
negative.text: Joined a red light sauna  for nerve & joint pain & laid on my stomach on a cot under 2 red light panels for 15 minutes 4 times a week- no sauna.  Did this for 16 months paying a monthly fee.  Then I picked up two 30 lb boxes separately from floor to waist high shelf & had a compression fracture T 12. (I had lifted these boxes at work for years repeatedly prior with no problem.) So I am proof that my spine did not benefit from red light.  Dexascan then showed osteopenia in spine & hip joints.  I am in my early 60's. (My hair did get thicker but 6 months after stopping red light, it went back to being thin)

transcript.summary: The speaker discusses the importance of taking vitamin D correctly, including the right dosage and pairing it with other nutrients, to achieve optimal health benefits.

positive.author: @Genetic_Modified_Viking
positive.text:
Thank you for not going AI.

I live in northern Michigan, so I sympathize on D levels.

I own a Sports Bar with axe throwing lanes (Three Cedars Restaurant in Millersburg).

If you’re in the area, stop in. We can throw axes and talk nutrition.

All the best to you in 2026.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @terrycahill8362
positive.text: Thanks Dr. Nick for no AI...solid info on Vit D3.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @VuurwerkLiefhebberij
positive.text: I've been taking 10,000 IU of vitamin D3 and 200mg of K2 in the morning with some peanut butter or other fat (cheese is also good) for a year now. I also take 465mg of elemental magnesium glycinate. As mentioned, it's important to take magnesium when taking vitamin D3.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @truth-1111
positive.text: i take 2000 iu of vitamin d supplement daily at 50 ngml vitamin d level when i took 5000 iu of vitamin d daily i didnt feel well at all daily and 10000 iu daily when i took i felt way worse so 2 thousand  iu of vitamin d daily in supplement form seems to work better for me so far this winter and i have very dark skin
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @BudKash
positive.text:
Hold on a second, Doc. I was under the impression that, although K2 is essential for D3 functionality, when taken together (as in a compound pill) they tend to conflict, because they compete for receptors. Have you heard this, and what is your opinion? I thought it's best to take D with Magnesium, but in terms of other co-factors, Zinc should not be taken simultaneously with Mag either, for instance.
You mentioned "mega-dosing", and this idea of doctors putting a patient on 50k iu daily for a brief period. Is it not true that D3 need to build up to the recommended blood levels, and to expedite this process it helps to take larger doses, especially if it was reduced to a maintenance dose thereafter? Some companies also market high-dose vit D, one brand specifically packages 50k iu caps, but as a weekly dosage, which seems reasonable if it takes time to accumulate and distribute. USRDA's are a bad joke.
All that being said, there are other important co-factors as well, especially for higher dosages of D3 (which your 10k iu daily could be considered) such as zinc, boron, vit A (beta carotene), and preferably a good B-complex also (like a methylated compound).
Thank you for an excellent, informative video.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @matthewwiecha8637
positive.text: Doc, "There's many vitamins" . Please,no, "There ARE many vitamins".
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @incrediblesubzero5405
positive.text: 25,000 iu D3k2 daily for autoimmune and inflammation
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @adnanhafiez4923
positive.text:
Taking 40k IU/day for 2+ months , to manage Psoriatic arthritis, It is working wonders for me.
Also taking K2, mag, Omega 3 and zinc.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @funeats8201
positive.text: My Vit D level was 33. I was getting sick about every 2 or 3 months and staying quite sick for an ENTIRE month. It was hell. At this time I was supplementing about 5,000 IU of vit D plus K2 and mag. Once I bumped it up to 10,000 IU per day and started using xylitol nasal spray, I stopped getting sick. I have had housemates get very sick, been in airplanes and hospitals and I miraculously never got sick. This was unheard of for me before. If anyone around me was remotely ill or if I went to a crowded place, I got sick. This has been life changing for me and I’m so grateful. It’s so nice to not feel like I’m just broken with a defective immune system. Watch The Dimming documentary. I think this is a huge factor in the epidemic of vit D deficiency but no one ever mentions it. They are deliberately blocking the sun’s rays.
positive.label: Positive

all.author: @BudKash
all.text:
Hold on a second, Doc. I was under the impression that, although K2 is essential for D3 functionality, when taken together (as in a compound pill) they tend to conflict, because they compete for receptors. Have you heard this, and what is your opinion? I thought it's best to take D with Magnesium, but in terms of other co-factors, Zinc should not be taken simultaneously with Mag either, for instance.
You mentioned "mega-dosing", and this idea of doctors putting a patient on 50k iu daily for a brief period. Is it not true that D3 need to build up to the recommended blood levels, and to expedite this process it helps to take larger doses, especially if it was reduced to a maintenance dose thereafter? Some companies also market high-dose vit D, one brand specifically packages 50k iu caps, but as a weekly dosage, which seems reasonable if it takes time to accumulate and distribute. USRDA's are a bad joke.
All that being said, there are other important co-factors as well, especially for higher dosages of D3 (which your 10k iu daily could be considered) such as zinc, boron, vit A (beta carotene), and preferably a good B-complex also (like a methylated compound).
Thank you for an excellent, informative video.
all.label: Positive

all.author: @funeats8201
all.text: My Vit D level was 33. I was getting sick about every 2 or 3 months and staying quite sick for an ENTIRE month. It was hell. At this time I was supplementing about 5,000 IU of vit D plus K2 and mag. Once I bumped it up to 10,000 IU per day and started using xylitol nasal spray, I stopped getting sick. I have had housemates get very sick, been in airplanes and hospitals and I miraculously never got sick. This was unheard of for me before. If anyone around me was remotely ill or if I went to a crowded place, I got sick. This has been life changing for me and I’m so grateful. It’s so nice to not feel like I’m just broken with a defective immune system. Watch The Dimming documentary. I think this is a huge factor in the epidemic of vit D deficiency but no one ever mentions it. They are deliberately blocking the sun’s rays.
all.label: Positive

transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The speaker emphasizes the importance of addressing high blood pressure through a specific method, slow breathing, which has been shown to be effective in lowering blood pressure and preventing stroke.

positive.author: @healthfitness03
positive.text: As a cardiovascular health specialist, I can say this video highlights a method that is often overlooked but strongly supported by clinical research. Techniques like this can activate the nervous system in a way that rapidly lowers blood pressure, sometimes more effectively than traditional aerobic exercise when done correctly. The key is consistency and proper technique. This is a safe, practical approach that many patients can start using immediately alongside medical guidance.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @WomanWellnessLab
positive.text: I’ve actually tried a very similar natural method, and I was surprised by how effective it was. I focused on simple daily habits like drinking warm water with natural ingredients, cutting down on salt, walking every day, and practicing slow breathing. Within a short time, my blood pressure readings became more stable, and I felt calmer and less tense. Of course, consistency is key, but natural approaches like this really can make a difference when done properly. Thanks for sharing this method—it aligns closely with what worked for me.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @busisiwemahlalela3661
positive.text: Amen, that's very true doctor. I was 220:198. Doctors were surprised how i made it to the clinic because people with this high blood pressure all ends up with a stroke? All I was doing was just pray, i didn't know about breathing. I was told to cut off salt, fatty and fried foods and do exercises. I took my treatment and exercised, in my next check up, they discovered I had a low sugar problem? My doctor was confused and ordered that I continue eating normally because he was not happy with the figures. Until I heard about the breathing system and I started doing them, I am now sitting at 112/98 and I am feeling strong. Writing from South Africa. ❤😅
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @kolaadewumi633
positive.text:
I want to thank you again --- I will need to always remember to do this because as you said---- knowledge without action is worthless.
May God's richest and best be yours.
Love you God bless you.
Our Lord Reigns.
Jesus is Lord .
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @SeniorHealthWisdom-686
positive.text: As a caregiver, I genuinely appreciate how you made this doable instead of overwhelming. The exact “6 breaths per minute” protocol at 25:36–26:11 (in 5… out 5… with a timer) is the first time I’ve seen it explained clearly enough that my loved one actually tried it—without feeling stressed. That practical step-by-step is what seniors need.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @lenaperez8929
positive.text: Deep breathing for 15 minutes breath in and breathe out slowly!! Am and afternoon than pm!! Everyday .  130 / 80 normal for seniors over 60 .
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @JosephAldridge-ti5nz
positive.text: Im my wifes careicer. Caregiver burnout IS a thing and needs to be faced head on. I had a small TIA last year and can't 'march' like I used to. She 81.  I 82. Semper vigilis.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @sandrak5511
positive.text:
I have been on a quest to lower my blood pressure for 2 years now. About a year after I retired it became high.  Spiking at times with systolic into 198/86.  Mostly the diastolic is not above 88.  That may be due to the vitamins I take. 
I will try this method of breathing.  I may start with 10 minutes and work up to 15 minutes.   I believe that we have blood pressure issues became of the toxins in our foods. I put an app called YUKA on my phone to check ingredients on everything I buy.  It is an absolute horror show of the crap in the foods at the grocery store. So it is back to basics!  No more canned biscuits with proplene glycol (yes anti freeze in that bisquet) will ever be in my grocery cart. Oh and it is in ice cream,  mouth wash etc. Anti freeze shuts down the kidneys.  Breathing and decent food are my new years resolution.  Thank you.
positive.label: Positive

positive.text:
This really puts into perspective how much is happening in the body behind the scenes—especially during sleep. A lot of people underestimate how critical sleep is, not just for energy but for actual repair, immune function, and inflammation control.

The connection between inflammation, recovery, and things like weight gain or fatigue makes a lot of sense when you look at it this way. It’s not just about diet or exercise alone—sleep and gut health play a huge role too.

Also liked how you kept it practical, focusing on simple habits and foods instead of overcomplicating things. Definitely a good reminder that small daily habits can have a big impact on long-term health.

positive.text: Super eye-opening! I love how you explain why some “healthy” teas (like hibiscus and detox blends) can actually backfire for seniors—especially when medications are involved. I also have a related video on my page about safer tea choices for older adults—feel free to check it out!

transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The speaker discusses the importance of tracking bone health in real-time, highlighting the limitations of traditional DEXA imaging and the benefits of using bone turnover markers.

positive.author: @dixiecrane1
positive.text: I am grateful that you are focusing on this challenging topic. It is discouraging to be treated by professionals who are not highly educated on bone density; they simply use a DEXA scan, then prescribe Calcium supplements plus a standard medication. I had to educate my oncologist about the need for D3 + K2. She googled it right while I was in her office and was surprised. (I learned about it from you!) I also got a vibration plate. My last DEXA showed I was back in the Osteopenia range. I'll keep doing it my way, thank you!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @lauragrady3227
positive.text: Dr. Doug, I have started using Function Health testing to manage my overall health proactively.  These two bone turnover tests are not currently offered as an add on test. I have requested these be added.  Thanks for all your videos!  I appreciate all the valuable information you provide.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @franrushie.510
positive.text: I will be 78 this year… a few years ago with a DEXA scan I was told my spine had a score of -4 and my hip had a score of -2… the doctors wanted to put me on medication…I told them I don’t take medication made with chemicals…I told the doctors I will look for natural remedies…I started an aggressive exercise program.. plus I switched to a meat, chicken, 🍗 fish, dairy diet…. I have had 3 fall since then to where my hip had a black and blue bruise and no broken bones..
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @susanc5314
positive.text: New here...68yo, diagnosed with osteoporosis in 2024. Under pressure I took alendronate for four months, then stopped. Started collagen daily, upped protein, severely lowered carbs, stopped my 1-a-day diet soda. I've eaten mostly whole foods for decades. I exercise although not always consistent. Had a follow up visit yesterday and my endocrinologist chastised me refusing to take more medication. She prescribed a new Dexa scan (which I'll do), bloodwork, and wants me to take Reclast as the side effects of the alendronate was part of my refusal to continue. Thank you for the video, now I know I can make improvements to bone density without meds. Wish I could find a better doctor.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @riarivers-hilton7985
positive.text: For more proper testings with trials we need more scientists (you know what I mean) and money!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @acem9302
positive.text: Dr. Doug. Thanks you very much for all the valuable info you are willing to share. For anybody else interested,  I got a doctor to order the lab tests and for the first time I am submitting them to my insurance. According to LabCorp the cost (with no insurance) is $190 for CTX and $274 for P1NP.  In the past I got them both through an online place for $225. Maybe it is wishful thinking but I am hoping my insurance will help me get a lower cost than the $225. Worst case scenario it will go towards my deductible.
positive.label: Positive

negative.author: @charlottejohnk6712
negative.text: My GP wanted me to take Fosamax but when she laughed at me when I told her I have lifted heavy weights for 40 years and she said lifting weights doesn’t help bones!   (I have a degree in exercise science) I decided although she’s very sweet osteoporosis isn’t her forte.   I then went to an endocrinologist and he put me on Fosamax and assured me that I would tolerate it and I would have a fabulous outcome.  I have never had joint pain.  Within 6 weeks I couldn’t bend forward because of horrible knee pain, my metatarsals were in horrible pain and I started having severe jaw pain.  I stopped taking Fosamax and will never take it again.  Needless to say, I am so glad that I found you!
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @RB-gt8bf
negative.text: I asked my doctor to order bone turnover markers, and I got the feeling she was not familiar.  She said she would get back with me.  She did get back with me and said no that insurance would not pay unless I was taking a medication to treat osteoporosis. I am going to bring this up again when I see her in a few weeks fro my annual checkup.
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @lindathompson4770
negative.text: This is all Way Over my head.  I haven't done density test scheduled in a couple of months.  Guess I'll see how it is from two years ago.
negative.label: Negative

neutral.author: @dulisse322
neutral.text: I'm doing REMS in March.
neutral.label: Neutral

transcript.summary: The speaker warns about the risks of certain morning habits on blood clot risk, particularly for adults over 60, and emphasizes the importance of awareness and small adjustments to the morning routine.

positive.author: @DrJohnSeniorHealth
positive.text:
Thank you so much for watching 💛
Blood clots rarely come from one big mistake — they build slowly from daily habits, especially in the morning.
Where are you watching from, and what is the very first thing you do after waking up? 🌅
Share your routine below — your comment might help someone protect their heart and brain.
If this video helped you, please like, subscribe, and share it with someone you care about.
A safer morning often leads to a healthier future 🌿
— The Dr John | Senior Health Team

transcript.summary: The overall tone of this transcript is hopeful and encouraging, with a focus on empowering seniors to take control of their health and reverse nerve damage.

highlightTokens.token: your body is designed to heal

positive.author: @shelleye9720
positive.text:
vitamins mentioned
B1 Benfotiamine
B12 methylcobalamin type  
ALA (Alpha lipid acid) the Stabilized R type
ALCAR=Acetylene L Carnitine

Avoid seed oils switch to Olive oil, Avocado oil, Coconut oil and butter.
Avoid liquid sugars.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @LutherSJ
positive.text: 2019 I had peripheral neuropathy so bad in my feet. I would sit on the edge of the time running the water as hot as I could. I spent my entire day in the bathroom smoking dope trying to stop the pain. At one point I asked my doctors to amputate my feet I saw a YouTube tutorial with Dr Mendel and he suggested alpha lipoic acid and acetyl L-Carnitine. I completely cut out all canola oil that's rapeseed oil which is banned in several countries and used in some countries as a pesticide. Every fast food restaurant in the United States uses canola oil and their deep fat fryer. I cured my peripheral neuropathy using YouTube. Thanks for the video
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @neesee456
positive.text:
Morning Fuel ☀️ 
Eggs 
- B1 (300 mg)
-B 12 (1000 mcg)
-ALA - (300) stabilized 
-ALCAR - (500) 
🌙 Evening walk -10 minutes 
(Uses glucose up so it does not stick to nerves)
🌝 Before bed 🛏️ 💤 -
Magnesium -400
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @GrandmaMel
positive.text: I lost all sensation in my feet after chemotherapy. 2 years later, my Oncologist told me I would never get it back. A respected doctor on YouTube recommended Magnesium L-Threonate. After taking it every day for 3 weeks, I woke up one morning with FULL SENSATION in both my feet! It has to be that specific kind of magnesium (L-threonate) it helps nerve conduction. I went out of the country, forgot my Mg, after 2 weeks off, I started to lose sensation. I started back on it after returning home and have had full sensation for 3 years!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @michael.w.salter
positive.text: I went on Amazon and ordered a bottle of "Alpha Lipoic Acid 600mg N-Acetyl Cysteine 600mg Acetyl L-Carnitine 500mg Benfotiamine 300mg - Nerve Support Supplement for Women and Men with Vitamin B1 B6 & B12 - Made in USA 90 Caps" by Clean Nutraceuticals.  My left foot has a varying degrees of numbness and sharp nerve pain, in my right foot is developing sharp nerve pain as well.  I had back surgery to relieve sciatic pain and numbness but I still have sharp nerve pain in my left leg. The doctor said I would have to live with the numbness in my left foot.  I'm going to try this supplement and hopefully it will help. Thank you for the video.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @PureBodySecrets
positive.text: This video by Dr. Michael Kent is an absolute lifesaver for anyone struggling with neuropathy! The way he explains the 'dying back' phenomenon and how nerves literally 'starve' due to sugar rust is both fascinating and easy to grasp. I am particularly impressed by the scientific distinction between standard B1 and the fat-soluble Benfotiamine; it’s a game-changer that most general advice overlooks. The comprehensive 'Nerve Revive Protocol' provided at the end is incredibly practical, offering a clear roadmap for recovery rather than just temporary pain management. It is heartening to see a medical professional address the root causes like nutrient depletion from common medications. This content gives so much hope to seniors who felt their numbness was permanent. Thank you, Dr. Kent, for this masterclass in nerve health and for empowering us with the right biochemical tools to heal. A must-watch for anyone over 60!
positive.label: Positive

Ordered the NMR tests myself starting 2 years ago when total particles were 1454 and small particles were 566 and improved diet as a result.  Taken no prescriptions in the last 2 years.  I watched a lot of videos to figure out the information you presented today.  Your information worked for me.  Age 84.

positive.author: @drdark7806
positive.text: Food isn’t ultimately that important as long as it’s not the SAD lol - it’s more about light water and magnetism - which food is just a form of light
positive.label: Positive

negative.author: @buppiano
negative.text: Sigh… and the parade of health diet gurus touting one thing today will change their minds tomorrow. We—like a ship  whose cargo is is shifting from port side to starboard with each wave—are tossed about  from one expert opinion to another. Eat GAPS…no, eat SCD!  Have broth for the gut! No bone broth… And at every pronouncement they are 100% sure they are correct… until they change their mind again. Paleo, Keto,  Ketovore, Carnivore… Plants will kill you, no, plants are medicine! Take supplements, no supplements… and do thousands of $ worth of testing. It’s  exhausting!
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @shellbell8062
negative.text: A bit misleading about GAPS as she says she stayed on soup for 2 years.  On GAPS intro you start eating foods other than soup at Stage 3 - many people get there in a couple of weeks to a month.  GAPS is actually vary varied, but you can't eat starches or grains.
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @dawnnwilliams2946
negative.text: I have always liked Ms Ruddick.  I can’t go along with  so much supplementation.  I think trying to feed microbes in the gut is not the way.  I don’t claim to be an expert but all I’ve seen about fiber seems like trying to manipulate the gut microbiome seems a waste of time.  I think the body works hard to run efficiently with whatever we give it.
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @lmp23612
negative.text: This woman comes across more as an influencer than a healthcare provider. She doesn’t seem to want y ty o provide specifics but just wants you to follow her and subscribe to her expensive program.
negative.label: Negative

negative.text: 400 bucks for a basic GI test. Have to be rich to get healthy.

transcript.summary: The speaker enthusiastically shares 10 high protein foods that are easy to prepare and pack for travel, emphasizing their convenience and nutritional benefits.

positive.author: @asmishler
positive.text: I've been getting into tuna packets this year as a way to just round out protein snack plates for lunch with basically zero flavor effort. It helps prevent me from having to think about "how" to have a can of tuna. 1 flavored tuna packet + cottage cheese and hot sauce + meat stick + apples + baby carrots + protein powder drink = 50+ grams of protein and good additional nutrients in maybe 3 minutes of prep.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @mariasheppard83
positive.text: Those are good ideas but we take full advantage of the free hotel breakfast such as bacon and eggs with cheese and fruit.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @dynomitenash8970
positive.text: Just poppin' in to say, OMG, your babies are TOO cute! Love all of your recipes. Have a great vacay.👋
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @fcsm5605
positive.text: I stocked up on all the quick protein snacks 🫠 bars, yogurt, shakes… still felt heavy and sluggish. It made zero sense. I was honestly tired of trying. Then I saw Eat like a Woman Protocol (by loren green) mentioned in a comment thread. 30 lbs gone, cravings calm, energy stable. kinda wild how simple shifts did more than “easy foods.”
positive.label: Positive

positive.label: Positive

Quietly perspective shifting, barely mentioned.

you'll need higher levels of B12

transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The video discusses the science behind sleep and fasting, presenting a positive and empowering message about understanding and working with one's biology.

highlightTokens.token: You're not crazy, and you're not alone.
Once you understand these mechanisms, you can work with them, not against them.
Sleeplessness isn't always a red flag.
Sometimes it's a sign your body is activating its most ancient survival systems instead of panicking over a few restless nights.

positive.author: @HealthMetabolicScience
positive.text:
If you’ve ever fasted and found yourself wide awake at 2–3 a.m., read this before panicking.

You’re not losing sleep because you’re hungry.
👉 You’re awake because your brain has switched into survival mode.

Without food, the brain increases orexin, cortisol, and adrenaline to keep you alert — just like our ancestors who stayed awake to hunt, not to sleep.
So feeling wired while fasting isn’t a mistake — it’s ancient biology doing its job.

The real question isn’t “Why can’t I sleep?”
👉 It’s “How do I work with my biology instead of fighting it?”

👉 When you fast, do you usually sleep less but feel sharper, or feel restless and anxious at night?
Share your experience below — you’re not alone.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @HealthScience-d9w
positive.text: I always thought my fasting insomnia meant something was wrong. But knowing it's orexin and survival coding? Mind blown. I'm not broken — I’m just activated.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @SheObeysAbbaYAH
positive.text: Glad youade this, because I thought I was going through something strange.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @HumanEnergyExplained
positive.text: Awesome, this video really got me thinking. I've been trying intermittent eating during the night, even when I think I'm tired. It's interesting to know that it's because our system is switching to survival mode. I never connected this before. Thanks for having the science behind it, this definitely makes me rethink my next approach to sleep.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @HealthMetabolicScience
positive.text:
You don’t need more discipline.
You need better alignment with how your body was designed to function.
That’s why these ancient habits work so well 👇
👉 https://youtu.be/-V-c19_hBuQ
positive.label: Positive

transcript.summary: The speaker discusses controlling insulin and blood sugar levels through dietary tweaks and biohacks, presenting a step-by-step plan to improve energy and reduce inflammation.

positive.author: @clarkesextonmusic
positive.text: The way Thomas delivers this information makes it clear that he takes the time to really understand and know what he is talking about. The amount of effort and time this fella puts in to know his craft and deliver the facts to us is incredible. Has changed my life and been an awesome part of my fitness journey! Cheers, Thomas!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @SpiritintheSky.
positive.text: He might be always trying to sell something, but you can't get away from the fact that he's a very good - and favourite - source of up to date information. Thank you so much for all those I've watched and no doubt will watch
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @waynesanders1406
positive.text: Magnesium counteracts calcium by shuttling it to bone/tendons/ligaments.  You can store calcium in those areas without magnesium.  If you have hardened arteries, it's probably because you've been magnesium deficient for a long time.  I use topical magnesium and have since 2017 or 2018.  It had an immediate effect on my energy and endurance in the gym.  I had amazing workouts.  Topically sprayed magnesium chloride is the way to go, hands down.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @ytpah9823
positive.text:
🎯 Key points for quick navigation:

💡 Controlling insulin, the "master hormone" regulating fat storage, is key to improving diet outcomes and overall energy.
🧠 Brain fog, stubborn body fat, and energy fluctuations may be linked to a single master hormone: insulin.
⚠️ Insulin management is crucial even for lean, fit, and healthy individuals, as silent struggles can rob energy and focus.
🗺️ The video will cover four key areas: how insulin works, a mineral to "turn off" insulin resistance, how a small amount of sugar can control glucose spikes, and an actionable timeline.
⚖️ Insulin is necessary but is also the "absorptive hormone" that helps absorb both glucose and fat, which can be problematic if not managed.
🛡️ Controlling insulin spikes is essential for proper nutrient absorption, glucose entering the muscle for energy, and preventing unnecessary fat storage.
🚫 Insulin stops fat loss by turning off hormone-sensitive lipase, keeping the body in an "absorptive mode" where stored fat cannot be released into the bloodstream.
🍽️ Periods of absorption are necessary for nutrients, but too much absorption can stress the body, cause excess inflammation, and lead to feeling unwell.
🛑 Too much insulin becomes inflammatory, promotes fat storage, causes "brain fogging," and can halt mitochondria from producing energy.
🥛 Calcium, typically associated with bones and teeth, plays an arguably more critical role in metabolism.
🚨 Calcium acts as the "go signal" or trigger that causes the pancreas's beta cells to release insulin into the bloodstream when blood sugar rises.
🔬 The rush of calcium into the cell is the physical trigger that initiates insulin release, a mechanism confirmed by molecular data.
✅ Supporting the initial calcium mechanism can lead to more efficient insulin release, allowing glucose to clear faster without the pancreas overworking.
💧 A simple takeaway is to sip on high-calcium mineral water (like Gerolsteiner or San Pellegrino) or consume A2 milk or Kefir throughout the day.
🍶 Kefir is recommended because it provides calcium along with the benefits of probiotics and fermented food.
💊 Adding calcium supplements should be avoided unless a specific need is identified, as they can increase coronary artery calcium and are not recommended by groups like the American Heart Association.
🐟 Getting calcium from food sources like sardines (with bone), Kefir, and cottage cheese is preferred over supplements.
💪 The "ultimate insulin hack" that doesn't require supplements or fancy food is muscle contractions—simply moving your body.
🏃 Muscle contraction, through movement like bodyweight squats, push-ups, or even a walk, can pull glucose out of the bloodstream without requiring insulin.
🚪 Your muscles have GLUT4 transporters (glucose gateways) that typically wait for insulin to open them, but muscle contraction bypasses this.
🔥 Contracting a muscle activates the energy sensor AMPK, which signals the GLUT4 gateways to immediately move to the cell surface and let glucose in for fuel, bypassing the need for insulin.
🚶 The easiest application is to move (e.g., 10-20 push-ups or a 10-15 minute walk) immediately after a meal instead of sitting down.
🎯 Immediate post-meal movement pulls sugar into muscles for fuel, preventing it from spiking insulin and interfering with fat burning.
🤯 Consuming a specific, small dose of the sugar fructose can surprisingly help manage overall blood sugar response.
📉 Adding just 7.5 grams of fructose (less than a tablespoon of honey) to a glucose drink resulted in a 14% lower blood sugar response in adults with Type 2 diabetes.
🌊 This mechanism works by leveraging the liver's function: the small dose of fructose activates the enzyme hepatic glucokinase, which pre-primes the liver to aggressively pull glucose out of the blood and store it.
🍓 A small handful of berries is an ideal way to implement this, as they provide the specific dose of fructose to prime the liver, along with fiber to slow absorption and polyphenols that also affect blood sugar.
⏱️ The fructose trick is an acute strategy, not an all-day practice; take one teaspoon of raw honey or a small handful of berries 15-20 minutes before a planned high-carb meal.
🍯 This small fructose dose pre-primes the liver to buffer the coming glucose hit from the high-carb meal.
✨ Manuka honey (specifically Manukora brand) is highlighted as a potent source of quality fructose, with added antimicrobial benefits due to a high MGO content.
🔬 The amino acid glycine offers a long-term approach to blood sugar management by building a stronger, more resilient system.
📈 A study showed that glycine acts as a signal to the pancreas to build more insulin-creating beta cells, potentially increasing their amount by 80% to 130%.
🏭 Glycine "invests in the factory" by building new "assembly lines" (beta cells) via the P13K and mTORC1 pathways, increasing the pancreas's reserve capacity.
🛡️ Increased beta cell reserve capacity gives the body more "horsepower" to effectively manage metabolic stress from a bad night's sleep or a cheat meal.
❄️ Cold exposure enhances glucose uptake in muscles, primarily due to the activation of brown adipose tissue (BAT).
🤎 Brown adipose tissue (BAT) is a special fat that activates when cold to burn fuel and create heat; its favorite fuel is glucose.
🛁 A cold shower makes your brown adipose tissue "suck" glucose out of your bloodstream to fuel its heat production.
⏰ Circadian timing is the final piece of the plan, as the body's machinery and insulin sensitivity are different in the morning versus the evening.
☀️ A study found that people who ate a higher proportion of calories and carbs earlier in the day had significantly better insulin sensitivity.
⬆️ Insulin receptors are naturally more sensitive in the morning and become more resistant as the day progresses because the body is designed to handle fuel while active.
🔄 If you are metabolically unhealthy, it is better to keep carbs low in the morning to capitalize on insulin sensitivity and tap into fat storage.
🌙 Metabolically unhealthy individuals may benefit from keeping carbs low all day and having some at night, which can help align circadian rhythms and potentially improve sleep.
🚶 A recommended start to the day is a 15-20 minute walk in sunlight, combining muscle contraction with resetting the circadian rhythm.
🏋️ The ideal time to have carbohydrates is during a workout (e.g., sipping watermelon juice), as exercise uses muscle contractions to absorb glucose without needing insulin.
🫐 To reset metabolism, keep carbohydrates low but include a small amount of polyphenols (e.g., a handful of blueberries with Greek yogurt) for overall insulin management.
⏰ The fructose primer (tiny bit of honey or raspberries) should be consumed 15-20 minutes before the largest meal, particularly if that meal includes carbohydrates.
💊 Mixing 3 to 5 grams of glycine powder into a small glass before the high-carb meal can also help prime the pancreas.
🌙 Immediately after the higher-carbohydrate dinner, go for another 10-15 minute walk to use muscle contraction to dispose of glucose into the cells.
🚿 End the day with a 30-60 second cold shower to activate glucose-hungry brown fat, potentially burning more fat overnight and programming mitochondria.
🔑 Stacking these principles allows you to architect your own energy and gain control over the master hormone insulin, instead of being a victim of blood sugar fluctuations.
💡 Individuals have incredible control over insulin and are not simply at the mercy of the food they eat; simple tweaks can influence how the hormone works.
📺 The speaker recommends watching another video on how he personally got rid of his insulin resistance and kept it away for over 13 years.

Made with HARPA AI
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @Bexxxtuff
positive.text: when i went to taiwan, everybody walks. in teipei, most locals in the city wear rubber shoes, very few wearing sandals or slippers, because they all walk anywhere. luckily before the trip, i prepared, i walked for an hour a day just to visit taiwan (my work is sitting all day). thats why theres no obese people in east asia... they walk A LOT!!
positive.label: Positive

positive.text: Thank you for this amazing content.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @deepost2604
positive.text: Age 83, female, I calculated my BMR recently.  It is 1340 calories for basic function x 30% daily activity=402 calories.  Yesterday FitBit indicates I burned 1752 calories.  In other words, I came close to 30% on that day.  This involved walking about 2.6 miles.  FitBit registered this as 323 active minutes. Do I believe this is accurate?  I’m NOT betting the store on this.  It’s just as close as I can come with the devices I have. My goal is to burn 1800 calories a day.  This gives me a bit of a framework to decide what extra activities I want to add.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @nicknorwitzMDPhD
positive.text:
I can’t believe how popular this Sardine Diet thing is turning out to be. Here are more resources and answers to FAQ (Mercury, Sardine Brand, etc.):

The original Sardine Diet Letter: https://staycuriousmetabolism.substack.com/p/sardine-fasting-the-ultimate-health?r=40ekz2 

Prior Video: https://youtu.be/_Th54GWd8WY 

Omega-3 and Thermogenesis Letter: Coming tomorrow 12/11/25 to StayCuriousMetabolism.com
 
Q: Aren’t Sardines high in mercury?
No. Sardines are a low mercury fish. You’d need to eat 77 servings of sardines to get the mercury in one serving of swordfish.

Q: What brand did you use for the sardine diet experiment?
Fishwife, preserved in lemon and extra virgin olive oil.

Q: What about BPA?
The brand was selected in part because it has BPA-free tins.
