transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The conversation revolves around a study on low-carb diets and their impact on performance and fat loss, with the speakers presenting evidence that contradicts traditional sports nutrition guidelines.

positive.author: @gordonanderson816
positive.text:
Totally Relevant .... I'm a 60 year old man that was in “good health” as the opinion of many doctors that I would see over the last 50 years. I have reversed many of my health conditions after I increased red meat protein with clean MTC fats including saturated fat & low PUFA fats, I also cut out refined carbohydrates and lowered my carbohydrates to under 50 grams.

If you do not have health problems you probably will not notice the effect of eating high carbs and sugar until you become diabetic.
My A1C went down after my doctor said it will not go down and will never go down….. after 3 years of low A1C my diagnosis of diabetes has been removed. My symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) have completely gone away with no surgery. I have more energy, no daytime sleep required. No muscle pain, much easier to climb into the truck. Allergies are much better.
PS: I have peripheral neuropathy in my feet that is getting better with time. Because I have this peripheral neuropathy in my feet it is very difficult for me to exercise. I did not have the energy to exercise. I drive a truck all day so I do not have time to exercise. I lost 40 lbs….. how can anyone say this is not Relevant?
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @jeffabfall6429
positive.text: This reminds me of the story of champion cyclist Greg LeMond. He snacked on sardines, not carb drinks or gels. While the others were bonking, he had sustained energy which won him Tour de France.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @drjazz777
positive.text:
🎯 Key points for quick navigation:

00:02 *🧪 The study challenges standard sports nutrition by testing high-carb vs very low-carb pre-exercise meals for performance.*
00:43 *🏃‍♂️ Focus on real-world middle distances: controlled 5K and 10K performance trials.*
01:12 *🎛️ Researchers tightly controlled confounders and compared a very low-carb meal to a guideline-style high-carb meal right before exercise.*
01:52:00 *🧬 Contrary to dogma, recommended carb intakes (e.g., 60–120 g/h) didn’t show the expected performance edge when variables were controlled.*
02:07:00 *🔁 Randomized crossover design: each subject did time trials on both pre-exercise meal conditions.*
02:46:00 *⚖️ Result: no difference in 5K/10K performance between high-carb and very low-carb pre-exercise meals.*
03:16:00 *🔥 Fuel use diverged: meals produced distinct carb vs fat oxidation profiles, yet performance was identical.*
05:19:00 *🧂 Presenter plug aside, electrolytes are suggested when fasting or cutting calories for comfort and adherence.*
06:14:00 *🎯 This was an acute pre-exercise meal test, not long-term diet—aimed at the immediate effect on performance.*
06:40:00 *🌙 Related evidence: some 5K studies show fasting doesn’t impair performance, though longer events may differ.*
09:28:00 *🧪 Prior trial: after 4 weeks keto adaptation, trained runners showed no decline in a 1-mile time trial or 6×800 m at >85% VO₂max.*
11:21:00 *🚀 Record fat burning: keto-adapted athletes oxidized >1.5 g/min (up to ~1.85 g/min) fat at high intensities—previously thought impossible.*
12:33:00 *🛡️ Long-duration test: Ironman athletes completed 6-week high-carb vs low-carb phases (with washout), tightly controlling training and calories.*
15:22:00 *🧷 Nuance for athletes: success on low-carb hinges on electrolytes, protein timing/amount, calories, and possibly strategic carbs.*
16:18:00 *🩺 Health angle: in a 2023 study, ~30% on high-carb showed prediabetic glucose—raising concerns even for lean, active athletes.*
17:28:00 *⚠️ “Fit but unhealthy” risk: frequent rapid-acting carbs can drive repeated glucose/insulin spikes despite high training volumes.*
19:02:00 *📊 Broader context: metabolic dysfunction is rising (including in youth), making diet quality a central performance-and-health issue.*
20:11:00 *🎯 Goal alignment: athletes shouldn’t trade health for performance—pursue both via appropriate dietary strategy.*
21:18:00 *🏆 Case example: Ironman champion Lionel Sanders reported reversing prediabetes by cutting ultra-processed carbs and then winning a world title.*
22:14:00 *🩹 Personal note: as a type 1 diabetic, the speaker underscores how glucose/insulin management impacts daily function and long-term health.*
22:43 *🧭 Athletes often prioritize performance over long-term health, sometimes following guidance that may not benefit metabolic health.*
23:21 *🏊‍♂️ Ironman elites skew older due to time demands, potentially making them more health-aware than younger pros.*
24:02 *🏋️ Younger athletes in high-intensity sports may ignore metabolic health in pursuit of gains.*
24:46 *🔮 Prediction: many athletes in their 20s–30s today could face metabolic problems within a decade.*
25:56 *📢 Message to athletes/coaches/clinicians: peak performance need not come at the expense of health.*
26:11 *😮 In a tightly controlled study, ~30% of lean, high-VO₂max runners showed glucose levels consistent with prediabetes.*
27:07 *🧪 The study controlled calories, weight, training, diet adherence (ketones), and CGM data with dietitian oversight.*
27:35 *🧰 Standard diagnostics (fasting glucose, OGTT, HbA1c) each have important limitations for athletes.*
29:22 *⏱️ Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) every 5 minutes across 4–6 weeks provided a richer picture of glycemic control.*
31:41 *📉 Age influences risk somewhat, but BMI and VO₂max weren’t strong predictors of fasted glucose status.*
32:07 *🍚 Diet—specifically daily carbohydrate amount—best predicted fasted glucose (baseline metabolic state).*
33:01 *🧩 Fasted glucose reflects underlying metabolism by stripping away meal/exercise “noise.”*
34:10 *🔁 Metabolically flexible individuals tolerate fasted training better; insulin-resistant people struggle more.*
35:02 *🧯 Pre-exercise carbs spike insulin, suppressing lipolysis and reducing fat oxidation capacity.*
35:56 *🧬 With fat use suppressed, muscle glycogen breakdown increases to sustain effort.*
37:22 *⚖️ Insulin is anabolic but context matters; keto adaptation lowers insulin and ketones can reduce protein breakdown.*
38:45 *🎯 Insulin acts more potently at fat cells than muscle, blocking fat release and pushing reliance on muscle glycogen.*
40:34 *⛽ Glycogen duration varies: ~4–5 hours at low intensity, but only ~1–2 hours at 70–80% VO₂max.*
41:30 *🧃 Frequent gels keep insulin elevated, potentially limiting fat use when glycogen is low—implications remain uncertain.*
43:08 *🧨 Ultra-endurance events are inherently catabolic; post-race labs often look severely stressed.*
43:52 *🧠 Regardless of diet, long efforts without fuel can cause hypoglycemia and neuroglycopenic symptoms.*
44:06 *🏁 Historical data (1920s Boston Marathon): runners finished hypoglycemic; providing carbs during races improved outcomes.*
45:00 *🧠 Century of data shows carbs aid performance mainly by preventing exercise-induced hypoglycemia.*
45:44 *⏳ Even on ketogenic diets, prolonged efforts (often >2 hours) still drive glucose down.*
46:10 *🧪 Across 160+ trials, hypoglycemia—not glycogen or carb-oxidation capacity—best explained carb benefits.*
46:40 *🩸 In controls, performance fell as glucose dipped; carbs worked by averting that dip.*
47:06 *🥑 Keto athletes still benefit from small intra-workout carbs to avoid bonking.*
47:36 *⚖️ Hypoglycemia occurred in most athletes during ≥2-hour efforts on both high- and low-carb diets.*
47:49 *🧮 Despite lower glucose on keto, total brain fuels (glucose+ketones+lactate) matched high-carb, with equal performance.*
48:45 *📉 “Minimal effective dose”: ~10 g carbohydrate per hour abolished hypoglycemia without altering glycogen use.*
49:12 *🚀 That tiny dose improved performance by ~22% across diets.*
49:24 *📊 Current guidelines (60–120+ g/h, even ~200 g/h) may overshoot what’s needed for performance.*
50:06 *🛑 Chronic high carb prescriptions risk long-term metabolic harm in some athletes.*
51:01 *🍚 Some guidance suggests ~1,000 g/day carbs for heavy training—flagged here as potentially dangerous.*
51:43 *⚖️ Double standard noted: high saturated fat is criticized, yet extreme carb loads get a pass.*
53:17 *🔄 Modeling that “requires” carbs at high intensities is challenged by real-world fat-oxidation data.*
53:59 *🏆 Example: world-class Ironman showed predominantly fat oxidation for ~half the record performance.*
54:39 *🔥 Keto-adapted athletes can sustain unprecedented fat oxidation even at >85% VO₂max.*
55:21 *🧭 High fat-oxidation capacity correlates with elite performance—even in high-carb athletes.*
55:49 *🧰 Practical keto tips: avoid unintended calorie deficit; keep diet well-formulated with adequate micronutrients.*
57:24 *📆 Adaptation window: commit ≥4 weeks; shorter trials can transiently underperform.*
57:53 *🧂 Electrolytes matter: early keto lowers insulin and sodium retention—supplement sodium to feel and perform better.*
01:00:11 *🎯 For long events, use the minimum carb dose that prevents hypoglycemia without suppressing fat oxidation.*
01:01:19 *🧬 Longer keto exposure may further upregulate fat-use enzymes—adaptation appears time-dependent.*
01:02:42 *🧯 Raising the “fat-burning zone”: keto can shift fat oxidation to higher intensities, aiding body-composition goals.*
01:03:39 *⚖️ After 4–6 weeks keto, fat-oxidation capacity can rise 3–4× while carb reliance falls (not to zero).*
01:05:32 *🏃 Slight, safe reductions in body mass during endurance efforts may marginally boost performance.*
01:06:01 *🧪 Plasma volume changes may influence performance, but the science remains limited and underexplored.*
01:06:14 *🧭 Many athletes fear experimenting with low-carb or ketogenic approaches due to performance anxiety or financial stakes.*
01:06:43 *🔍 Trying alternative diets can reveal unexpected health or performance benefits—avoid assumptions until personally tested.*
01:06:57 *🧓 Younger athletes often ignore long-term health, assuming vitality lasts indefinitely.*
01:07:23 *🚬 Health outcomes accumulate like smoking—risk comes from repeated choices over years, not isolated actions.*
01:07:50 *🕰️ Sustainable health and longevity depend on consistent habits, not short-term fixes or trends.*
01:08:04 *🌱 People should periodically reassess diet and lifestyle as goals evolve, balancing performance with long-term well-being.*
01:08:18 *🌐 Dr. Andrew Koutnik shares free resources at andwilk.com and on social platforms (@akoutnikphd) for ongoing education.*
01:08:32 *💡 He emphasizes continual learning—science evolves, and so should our understanding of nutrition and metabolism.*

positive.author: @ByanGwok
positive.text: This is one of the biggest questions I recently facing as an ultra runner. I have been experiencing last 2 years with limiting fuel as much possible during long duration run. I recently figure out I only need start eating at 2hr mark. And then half an orange for maybe next hour depends on the weather.  Normally my heart rate flat out after 3 hrs and normally stay at low zone 3 comfortabily the rest of the day. At the zone 3 state my experience tell me that an orange every hour is all I need. I aware most of the energy problem is to do with salt deficiency instead, especially hot weather mindful with magnesium(muscle tension) and potassium(elevated heart rate) deficiency. By saying this, I also received an adviced by my friend as a experience ultra runner telling me I need 60g of carb. every hour, and that is like 4 oranges per hour. Artificial products never work on me, so I decided to stick to what I experimented. Just shared some of the discovery.
positive.label: Positive

all.author: @gordonanderson816
all.text:
Totally Relevant .... I'm a 60 year old man that was in “good health” as the opinion of many doctors that I would see over the last 50 years. I have reversed many of my health conditions after I increased red meat protein with clean MTC fats including saturated fat & low PUFA fats, I also cut out refined carbohydrates and lowered my carbohydrates to under 50 grams.

If you do not have health problems you probably will not notice the effect of eating high carbs and sugar until you become diabetic.
My A1C went down after my doctor said it will not go down and will never go down….. after 3 years of low A1C my diagnosis of diabetes has been removed. My symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) have completely gone away with no surgery. I have more energy, no daytime sleep required. No muscle pain, much easier to climb into the truck. Allergies are much better.
PS: I have peripheral neuropathy in my feet that is getting better with time. Because I have this peripheral neuropathy in my feet it is very difficult for me to exercise. I did not have the energy to exercise. I drive a truck all day so I do not have time to exercise. I lost 40 lbs….. how can anyone say this is not Relevant?
all.label: Positive

all.author: @jeffabfall6429
all.text: This reminds me of the story of champion cyclist Greg LeMond. He snacked on sardines, not carb drinks or gels. While the others were bonking, he had sustained energy which won him Tour de France.

transcript.summary: The speaker critiques outdated weight loss tips and promotes a balanced approach to nutrition, emphasizing the importance of eating the right types of foods to achieve sustainable weight loss.

positive.author: @amythorpe9257
positive.text: I lost 30 lbs by counting calories, but I was eating a high fat, high carb diet, just fewer overall calories than I had in the past. Almost 10 years later, I've gained back half of it. Now I'm concentrating on PFF and lifting weights. I haven't seen the scale move yet (3 months in), but I feel better. Sometimes, I'm tempted to go back to calorie counting, since it worked for me before, but it's not sustainable and it lowered my metabolism when I lost so much muscle the first time. I'm determined to stick with the current plan for a few more months. Thanks for the motivation.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @homomorphic
positive.text:
Lost 20% of my bodyweight in 90 days 4 years ago. Still 8.4% body fat, but my weight has gone up with muscle. I've added 4 lbs of muscle in the last 6 weeks.

I fast 18 hours every day.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @ErinPartrick
positive.text: I think when your sleeping at night...you sort-of are Intermittent fasting. I guess you have to do what feels right for you. However For myself I don't really Think a calorie is just a calorie. I do think it matters what TIME YOUR CONSUMING THE CALORIES.!!! I feel like when i start Raiding the Ice-Box after a certain time...for me it's around 6:00 P.M. I think it matters the next day. Think about it your not Active in the Evening...your Body needs to wind Down for sleeping. So I think maybe once or Twice you can get away with it. But if you eat or (Snack) at night it could add up to Gaining...That's what i think...thanks.!!!
positive.label: Positive

transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The overall tone of this video is positive and encouraging, with the author offering practical advice and tools to help viewers manage their emotions and develop resilience in the face of family stress.

positive.author: @MrZenToad
positive.text: A friend of mine invited me to spend Thanksgiving with her family. It was really great. No criticism, no unwinnable situations, no parental temper tantrums. After going home, I spent the rest of the weekend alone.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @user-ll6hk8ls5c
positive.text: Wow! Your ability to validate the experience of family AND explain the science behind it AND give us tools to take wherever we go to encourage us to rewire/ rewrite our brains for the future…best gift ever. Thank you!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @RobG1729
positive.text:
A day or two into a visit with family, my mother says, "Someone is upset with you."
Rather than becoming anxious and asking for details, I replied, "It wouldn't be a visit home if I didn't.  I can cross that off my to-do list."
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @AntonioYuri808
positive.text: My Dad likes to make comments about my weight jokingly and then proceeds to tell me "Finish your food" during dinner. I understand jokes are his way to bond and the not wasting food bit is contributing to the trauma of food scarcity he faced growing up in the Philippines. I need to remember these are why he does what he does. With patience and setting boundaries, I hope to not instantly get upset with my Dad
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @maddihuss6359
positive.text: Dr Tracey Marks knows what I need better than I do 😭 mentally preparing for Christmas 🫣
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @bartoszkozlak1440
positive.text: It's actually very  relieving and motivating we can rewrite those childhood biases
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @mariatlp1954
positive.text: I need to practice this with my mom, not only for the holidays but anytime we gather!!! Thanks a million for the video!!!👏👏
positive.label: Positive

negative.author: @Dbj007
negative.text: Family time makes my anxiety go from 0-10 in a minute and I’m a mess and in hyper focus the entire time I’m with them
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @iceyone7137
negative.text:
Tracey Marks is kindof a healthcare hero for making these videos.

If any "family" causes one discomfort then that's a toxic family that's only a family in the primitive sense of the word. So many people are cursed to be born in unwanted families, it's the biggest dice roll in life. It makes me so resentful not just for myself but for others too.

What I wish these psychologists taught more isn't only how to be resilient but how to fight. You need to fight too, even if it risks you becoming insane.
negative.label: Negative

negative.author: @dougjowdy4285
negative.text: It is not even worth the energy anymore to use these techniques to cope with the family. They are not fun to be around and will continue to act out their trauma. And it's not for me to learn to pivot/tap dance anymore when the situation has no value to me whatsoever. Detaching with love works best. And when indicated, in a loving manner be honest about my experience in response to their mindlessness. They cannot and will not accept that honesty is an act of love. If I can't be honest with someone, I'm left in a position feeling vulnerable. Both my ANS and adrenals find that to be a fools game to engaging in the mental gymnastics required in the face of people who are emotionally blind. I believe strongly in what you shared, it will help immensely in other situations.
negative.label: Negative

transcript.label: Positive
transcript.summary: The speaker discusses their personal approach to healthspan, emphasizing the importance of consistency, focusing on foundational habits, and being cautious of trendy supplements. They highlight the benefits of creatine and omega-3 supplements, and discuss their use of medications for preventative purposes.

positive.author: @emulare1110
positive.text: I do HIIT ( High Intensity Internet Training). Watching videos at 2X speed of other people exercising has been great for my health!
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @sayanmukherjee2417
positive.text: Astaxanthin in consideration.. Happy new year 🎉
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @eja2512
positive.text: Matt, I’ve always appreciated your skepticism on the categories people are certain on; it’s refreshing and I feel people should have a little more of this. Thank you for keeping it simple.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @DanielHarrell-y9z
positive.text: Thanks for all the information. Your view points are valued.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @ZeitgeistHomer
positive.text: Thank you for talking about Novos and your relationship with them.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @rickyf
positive.text: This is one of your best podcasts. The information is well presented and valuable to me.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @michaeldiener5053
positive.text: I agree that it is difficult to discern a benefit from supplementation, especially when you are trying different things. Going on my own experiene, curcumin (turmeric) + pepper is said to be good for arthritis, and I have found the incidents of flare-up at that thumb/wrist joint (CMC?) have been reduced, and I do feel looser in the hip joints when hiking. I also feel like I have had fewer of those "why was I going upstairs?" moments since I started taking creatine at a 10 mg level. These are both fairly low cost supplements. I also take a few others where my food tracker said I was consistently low, like fish oil, magnesium/zinc/D, and choline. I do add chia and flax to some foods like overnight oats and fruit jam, but I don't think of this as supplementation.
positive.label: Positive

positive.author: @MiddleSky
positive.text:
Thanks for this, I am getting more interested in lifestyle factors for healthspan. Supplements are expensive and don't really do much except for a few. 
The social aspect is a weak point for me, too so I look forward to hearing more on how you work on it. There are articles out there with topic suggestions on having deeper conversations, I think I'll look some up again so I can actually talk to a friend when we do a road trip this year, instead of just daydreaming the whole trip like I did last time. Uncomfortable, but I'll try to think of it like a muscle I'm strengthening.
positive.label: Positive

negative.author: @davin8r
negative.text: Matt, I hope you're aware empagliflozin raises hematocrit significantly which is additive to the increase from testosterone.  There is a recent published paper warning about this.  It's a well-established class effect of all the SGLT2 inhibitors.
negative.label: Negative

neutral.author: @mikehouston8884
neutral.text: Brian Kennedy mentioned the acute effect on exercise when he took Rejuvant with sublingual NAD+ from IX Biopharna.
neutral.label: Neutral
